1.synthesis:
Psalm 82 is the Psalter's most concentrated engagement with the divine council theology: the idea that YHWH presides over an assembly of divine beings who exercise delegated authority over the nations. The opening verse — 'God has taken his place in the divine council; in the midst of the gods he holds judgment' (v. 1) — uses the Hebrew bĕʿădat-ʾēl ('in the assembly/council of God') and bĕqereb ʾĕlōhîm ('in the midst of the gods'), the same worldview reflected in Ps 89:7 and Job 1:6. These 'gods' (ʾĕlōhîm) are the members of the divine council — beings with real but derived authority — who are here indicted for their failures.

The indictment of vv. 2–4 is specific: 'How long will you judge unjustly and show partiality to the wicked? Give justice to the weak and the fatherless; maintain the right of the afflicted and the destitute. Rescue the weak and the needy; deliver them from the hand of the wicked.' The divine council members have failed at the most fundamental task of governance: justice for the vulnerable. They have shown partiality to the wicked and neglected the classic covenant categories of the protected class — weak, fatherless, afflicted, destitute, needy. The cosmic injustice produced by this failure is described in v. 5: 'They have neither knowledge nor understanding, they walk about in darkness; all the foundations of the earth are shaken.' Cosmic stability depends on just governance; when the governors fail at justice, the very foundations of the earth are destabilized.

The divine judgment oracle of vv. 6–7 — 'I said, “You are gods, sons of the Most High, all of you; nevertheless, like men you shall die, and fall like any prince.”' — is YHWH's sentence on the unjust gods. Their divine status is acknowledged ('you are gods, sons of the Most High') but revoked by their failure: they will die like mortals. The psalm closes with the congregation's petition in v. 8: 'Arise, O God, judge the earth; for you shall inherit all the nations!' — a call for YHWH to take direct possession of what the unjust gods have forfeited through their failure.
1.historical_context: The divine council theology reflects the ancient Near Eastern cosmology common to Israel's environment, in which great gods presided over assemblies of lesser divine beings. Psalm 82 demythologizes this tradition: YHWH sits as the sovereign judge over whatever beings have been granted authority, and those beings are accountable to the same moral standard — justice for the vulnerable — that YHWH himself exemplifies. The psalm's later interpretation ranged from the 'gods' being human judges (Exod 21:6, 22:8) to the fallen divine beings of Gen 6:1–4 to the patron angels of the nations (Deut 32:8 in the LXX/DSS reading).
1.literary_structure: The psalm's eight verses form a complete judicial proceeding: court session established (v. 1) → charge / indictment (vv. 2–4) → diagnosis of cosmic consequences (v. 5) → sentence (vv. 6–7) → congregational petition for direct divine rule (v. 8). The movement from established court to sentence to petition is remarkably compressed, covering the entirety of divine judgment and its human implications in eight verses.
1.theological_application: The measure by which the council-gods are judged — justice for the weak, fatherless, afflicted, destitute, and needy (vv. 3–4) — is the same measure by which all human authority is evaluated. The psalm teaches that power exists for the protection of the vulnerable, and that the failure of those in authority to protect the weak is a cosmic failure with cosmic consequences: 'all the foundations of the earth are shaken' (v. 5). This is the theological basis for prophetic critique of unjust governments and unjust judges: they are accountable to a standard higher than themselves, and their failure has consequences beyond themselves.
1.christology: Jesus quotes v. 6 — 'you are gods' — in John 10:34–35 in a qal-wachomer argument: if the Scripture called mere human judges 'gods' (because the word of God came to them), how much more appropriate is it for Christ — whom the Father consecrated and sent into the world — to claim divine sonship. The 'one who shall inherit all the nations' of v. 8 — the God who arises to judge the earth — is identified in the NT with Christ, who has received 'all authority in heaven and on earth' (Matt 28:18) and to whom 'every knee shall bow' (Phil 2:10).
